column1: NIST IR 8477-Based Set Theory Relationship Mapping (STRM)

column3: Focal Document Element (FDE)
column6: STRM Rationale
column11: CJIS Control Description
column12: Notes

column3: Covered entities must ensure the CIA of all electronic protected health information and protect against reasonably anticipated threats.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(a) requires covered entities to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all ePHI and protect against anticipated threats. CJIS 5.17.PL-8 requires development of security and privacy architectures that describe requirements and approaches for protecting CIA of organizational information. Both mandate a foundational organizational commitment to CIA principles for sensitive information.
column11: Develop security and privacy architectures for the system that describe the requirements and approach to be taken for protecting the confidentiality integrity and availability of organizational information.
column12: HIPAA covers ePHI broadly; CJIS covers CJI. Both establish CIA as the foundational security objective. HIPAA additionally covers patient health information in all forms; CJIS additionally covers criminal justice information in law enforcement contexts.

column3: Covered entities must ensure the CIA of all electronic protected health information and protect against reasonably anticipated threats.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(a) requires protection against reasonably anticipated threats to ePHI security. CJIS 5.19.RA-3 requires conducting a risk assessment identifying threats and vulnerabilities and determining likelihood and magnitude of harm. Both require proactive identification and mitigation of threats to sensitive information.
column11: Conduct a risk assessment including identifying threats to and vulnerabilities in the system; determining the likelihood and magnitude of harm from unauthorized access.
column12: HIPAA additionally covers operational and administrative threats to ePHI; CJIS additionally covers threats specific to criminal justice information systems.

column3: Covered entities may use any security measures that allow reasonable and appropriate implementation taking into account size complexity capabilities costs and probability of risks.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(b) requires covered entities to consider size, complexity, capabilities, costs, and risk probability when selecting security measures. CJIS 5.19.RA-3 requires risk assessments that determine the likelihood and magnitude of harm to guide security decisions. Both require risk-informed and context-sensitive security decision making.
column11: Conduct a risk assessment including identifying threats to and vulnerabilities in the system; determining the likelihood and magnitude of harm from unauthorized access.
column12: HIPAA additionally provides a flexibility framework for covered entities to select measures based on organizational factors; CJIS additionally mandates specific assessment frequencies and remediation timeframes.

column3: A covered entity must comply with all standards in §164.308 §164.310 §164.312 §164.314 and §164.316.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(c) requires covered entities to comply with all enumerated security standards. CJIS 5.17.PL-2 requires development of security and privacy plans consistent with the enterprise architecture that describe controls planned for meeting security requirements. Both establish mandatory compliance frameworks for protecting sensitive information.
column11: Develop security and privacy plans for the system that are consistent with the organization's enterprise architecture and describe the controls in place or planned for meeting the security and privacy requirements.
column12: HIPAA standards apply to ePHI; CJIS standards apply to CJI. Both represent comprehensive compliance frameworks requiring documented adherence to enumerated security controls.

column3: Implementation specifications are required or addressable; covered entities must implement required specifications and assess addressable ones.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(d) establishes a tiered system of required and addressable implementation specifications requiring documentation when an addressable specification is not implemented. CJIS 5.17.PL-11 requires tailoring the selected control baseline by applying specified tailoring actions. Both allow risk-based tailoring of security controls with documentation requirements.
column11: Tailor the selected control baseline by applying specified tailoring actions.
column12: HIPAA distinguishes between required and addressable specifications; CJIS uses a formal tailoring process. HIPAA additionally requires documentation of why addressable specifications are not implemented; CJIS additionally defines a structured baseline selection process.

column3: Security measures must be reviewed and modified as needed to continue providing reasonable and appropriate protection of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.306(e) requires ongoing review and modification of security measures to maintain appropriate protection of ePHI. CJIS 5.17.PL-2 requires reviewing system security and privacy plans at least annually and updating them to address changes. Both mandate continuous maintenance and updating of security controls to respond to changing conditions.
column11: Develop security and privacy plans for the system that are consistent with the organization's enterprise architecture; review the system security and privacy plans at least annually.
column12: Both require periodic review and update of security measures/plans in response to environmental or operational changes. HIPAA frames this as ongoing maintenance of safeguards; CJIS frames it as annual plan review and update.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to prevent detect contain and correct security violations.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(i) requires policies and procedures to prevent, detect, contain, and correct security violations. CJIS 5.3.IR-1 requires development of agency-level incident response policy and procedures addressing preparation, detection, containment, and recovery. Both require comprehensive security management frameworks addressing the full lifecycle of security events.
column11: Develop document and disseminate agency-level incident response policy and procedures addressing purpose scope roles responsibilities and compliance.
column12: Both require formal policies and procedures for managing the full lifecycle of security events including prevention detection containment and correction/recovery.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to prevent detect contain and correct security violations.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(i) requires security management policies and procedures. CJIS 5.5.AC-1 requires development of agency-level access control policy and procedures. Both mandate formal documented policy frameworks as the foundation of security management.
column11: Develop document and disseminate to organizational personnel with access control responsibilities: agency-level access control policy and procedures to facilitate the implementation of the access control policy.
column12: HIPAA covers broad security management; CJIS AC-1 covers access control specifically. HIPAA additionally addresses incident containment and correction; CJIS additionally provides specific access control implementation guidance.

column3: Conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to the CIA of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A) requires accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to ePHI CIA. CJIS 5.19.RA-3 requires conducting a risk assessment identifying threats and vulnerabilities and determining likelihood and magnitude of harm. Both require systematic analysis of risks to sensitive information systems.
column11: Conduct a risk assessment including identifying threats to and vulnerabilities in the system; determining the likelihood and magnitude of harm from unauthorized access use disclosure disruption modification or destruction.
column12: Both require comprehensive risk and vulnerability assessments covering CIA impacts. HIPAA specifies assessment of ePHI; CJIS specifies assessment of CJI systems.

column3: Conduct an accurate and thorough assessment of potential risks and vulnerabilities to the CIA of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A) requires vulnerability assessment. CJIS 5.19.RA-5 requires monitoring and scanning for vulnerabilities at least monthly. Both address identification of vulnerabilities in information systems holding sensitive data.
column11: Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities in the system and hosted applications at least monthly and when new vulnerabilities potentially affecting the system are identified and reported.
column12: HIPAA requires a comprehensive risk analysis as a periodic assessment; CJIS requires continuous vulnerability scanning. HIPAA additionally addresses broader risk probability and criticality; CJIS additionally specifies remediation timeframes by severity.

column3: Implement security measures sufficient to reduce risks and vulnerabilities to a reasonable and appropriate level.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(B) requires implementing security measures sufficient to reduce risks to a reasonable and appropriate level. CJIS 5.19.RA-7 requires responding to findings from security assessments in accordance with organizational risk tolerance. Both require risk-based implementation of security measures to reduce identified risks.
column11: Respond to findings from security and privacy assessments monitoring and audits in accordance with organizational risk tolerance.
column12: Both require taking risk-informed action to reduce identified vulnerabilities and risks to an acceptable level. HIPAA applies to ePHI; CJIS applies to CJI.

column3: Implement security measures sufficient to reduce risks and vulnerabilities to a reasonable and appropriate level.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(B) requires risk reduction measures. CJIS 5.19.RA-5 requires vulnerability remediation within defined timeframes. Both require action to mitigate identified security vulnerabilities.
column11: Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities in the system and hosted applications at least monthly; remediate legitimate vulnerabilities within defined timeframes.
column12: HIPAA requires general risk reduction measures; CJIS specifies quantitative remediation timeframes by severity. HIPAA additionally addresses broader risk management strategies; CJIS additionally requires automated scanning tools.

column3: Apply appropriate sanctions against workforce members who fail to comply with security policies and procedures.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(C) requires appropriate sanctions against workforce members who fail to comply with security policies and procedures. CJIS 5.12.4 requires a formal sanctions process for personnel failing to comply with established information security policies and procedures. Both require formal enforcement mechanisms for security policy compliance.
column11: The agency shall employ a formal sanctions process for personnel failing to comply with established information security policies and procedures.
column12: Both require formal documented sanctions processes for workforce members who fail to comply with security policies. Direct semantic equivalence.

column3: Implement procedures to regularly review records of information system activity such as audit logs access reports and security incident tracking reports.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D) requires regular review of information system activity including audit logs and access reports. CJIS 5.4.AU-6 requires weekly review and analysis of system audit records for indications of inappropriate or unusual activity. Both require regular review and analysis of audit records to detect security anomalies.
column9: 5.4.AU-6 Audit Record Review Analysis and Reporting
column11: Review and analyze system audit records weekly for indications of inappropriate or unusual activity and the potential impact; report findings to organizational personnel with audit review and information security responsibilities.
column12: Both require regular review of audit records and reports for security monitoring. HIPAA specifies review of audit logs, access reports, and incident tracking; CJIS specifies weekly review frequency and reporting requirements.

column3: Implement procedures to regularly review records of information system activity such as audit logs access reports and security incident tracking reports.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D) requires review of system activity records. CJIS 5.4.AU-2 requires identifying types of events for logging including authentication, file use, and user management events. Both require systematic logging and review of system activity.
column11: Identify the types of events that the system is capable of logging in support of the audit function including authentication file use user/group management and operational transactions.
column12: HIPAA focuses on review of existing records; CJIS AU-2 focuses on defining what to log. HIPAA additionally covers access reports and incident tracking; CJIS additionally specifies event types and annual update requirements.

column3: Identify the security official responsible for development and implementation of security policies and procedures.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(2) requires identification of a security official responsible for security policies and procedures. CJIS 5.6.IA-1 requires designating an individual with security responsibilities to manage identification and authentication policy and procedures. Both require designation of responsible individuals for security program management.
column11: Designate an individual with security responsibilities to manage the development documentation and dissemination of the identification and authentication policy and procedures.
column12: HIPAA requires a single designated security official for the entire security program; CJIS IA-1 designates responsibility for a specific control family. HIPAA is broader in scope; CJIS additionally covers specific IA policy domains.

column3: Identify the security official responsible for development and implementation of security policies and procedures.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(2) requires identification of a security official. CJIS 5.3.IR-1 requires designating an individual with security responsibilities to manage incident response policy. Both require formal assignment of security management responsibility.
column11: Designate an individual with security responsibilities to manage the development documentation and dissemination of the incident response policy and procedures.
column12: HIPAA establishes a single security official; CJIS establishes designated responsibility across multiple control families. Both require formal assignment of security accountability.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to ensure appropriate ePHI access and prevent unauthorized access.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(i) requires policies and procedures to ensure appropriate ePHI access and prevent unauthorized access by workforce members. CJIS 5.5.AC-1 requires agency-level access control policy addressing roles, responsibilities, and compliance. Both require formal access control policies governing workforce access to sensitive information.
column11: Develop document and disseminate agency-level access control policy that addresses purpose scope roles responsibilities management commitment coordination among organizational entities and compliance.
column12: Both require formal access control policies ensuring appropriate access by authorized personnel and preventing unauthorized access to sensitive information.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to ensure appropriate ePHI access and prevent unauthorized access.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(i) requires workforce access policies. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires defining and documenting types of accounts allowed and assigning account managers to control system access. Both require systematic management of workforce access authorizations.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed and specifically prohibited for use within the system; assign account managers; specify authorized users and access authorizations for each account.
column12: HIPAA focuses on policy-level workforce access controls; CJIS AC-2 provides detailed technical account management requirements. HIPAA additionally addresses workforce clearance procedures; CJIS additionally specifies account lifecycle management procedures.

column3: Implement procedures for authorization and/or supervision of workforce members who work with ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(ii)(A) requires procedures for authorization and supervision of workforce members accessing ePHI. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires approval processes for account creation and management based on valid access authorization. Both require formal authorization processes before granting access to sensitive information.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed; require approvals by organizational personnel with account management responsibilities for requests to create accounts; authorize access to the system based on a valid access authorization.
column12: Both require formal authorization and approval processes before workforce members receive access to sensitive information. HIPAA covers ePHI access authorization and supervision; CJIS covers account management authorization.

column3: Implement procedures to determine that access of a workforce member to ePHI is appropriate.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(ii)(B) requires procedures to determine whether workforce access to ePHI is appropriate. CJIS 5.12.1 requires state and national fingerprint-based record checks prior to granting access to CJI for all personnel with unescorted access. Both require pre-access screening to ensure appropriateness of access.
column11: State of residency and national fingerprint-based record checks shall be conducted prior to granting access to CJI for all personnel who have unescorted access to unencrypted CJI.
column12: HIPAA requires determination of access appropriateness without specifying methods; CJIS requires specific fingerprint-based record checks. HIPAA additionally covers ongoing appropriateness assessment; CJIS additionally defines specific disqualifying criteria.

column3: Implement procedures to determine that access of a workforce member to ePHI is appropriate.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(ii)(B) requires determining appropriate access. CJIS 5.5.AC-6 requires employing least privilege principles. Both address ensuring only appropriate access is granted to sensitive information systems.
column11: Employ the principle of least privilege allowing only authorized accesses for users that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.
column12: HIPAA focuses on procedures to verify access appropriateness; CJIS focuses on technical implementation of least privilege. Both ensure workforce access is limited to what is appropriate and necessary.

column3: Implement procedures for terminating access to ePHI when employment ends or access is determined inappropriate.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(ii)(C) requires procedures to terminate ePHI access when employment ends. CJIS 5.12.2 requires immediate termination of access to local agency systems when personnel are terminated. Both require prompt revocation of access upon personnel termination.
column11: Upon termination of personnel by an interface agency the agency shall immediately terminate access to local agency systems with access to CJI and provide notification to ensure access to state and other agency systems is terminated.
column12: Both require immediate termination of system access when personnel leave the organization. HIPAA covers ePHI systems; CJIS covers CJI systems with additional notification requirements for state systems.

column3: Implement procedures for terminating access to ePHI when employment ends or access is determined inappropriate.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(3)(ii)(C) requires termination procedures. CJIS 5.5.AC-2(3) requires disabling accounts within one week of inactivity termination or policy violation. Both require timely deactivation of accounts when access is no longer appropriate.
column11: Disable accounts within one week when the accounts have expired, are no longer associated with a user, are in violation of organizational policy, or have been inactive for 90 calendar days.
column12: HIPAA focuses on employment termination triggering access revocation; CJIS provides broader account disabling criteria including inactivity. Both ensure timely removal of access when no longer appropriate.

column3: Implement policies and procedures for authorizing access to ePHI consistent with applicable requirements.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(i) requires policies and procedures for authorizing access to ePHI. CJIS 5.5.AC-1 requires agency-level access control policy and procedures. Both require formal documented policies governing access authorization to sensitive information.
column11: Develop document and disseminate to organizational personnel with access control responsibilities: agency-level access control policy that addresses purpose scope roles responsibilities management commitment coordination among organizational entities and compliance.
column12: Both require documented access control policies and procedures as the foundation for information access management. HIPAA specifies ePHI; CJIS specifies CJI.

column3: Implement policies and procedures for authorizing access to ePHI consistent with applicable requirements.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(i) requires information access management policies. CJIS 5.5.AC-3 requires enforcement of approved authorizations for logical access. Both require policies and technical controls ensuring only authorized individuals access sensitive information.
column11: Enforce approved authorizations for logical access to information and system resources in accordance with applicable access control policies.
column12: HIPAA focuses on policy and procedural access management; CJIS AC-3 focuses on technical enforcement of access authorizations. HIPAA additionally covers administrative controls; CJIS additionally specifies technical enforcement mechanisms.

column3: Health clearinghouses must implement policies protecting ePHI from unauthorized access by the larger organization.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(A) requires health clearinghouses to protect ePHI from unauthorized access by larger parent organizations through isolation policies. CJIS 5.10.SC-4 requires prevention of unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources. Both address isolation and separation of sensitive information from unauthorized access within shared or larger system environments.
column11: Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.
column12: HIPAA focuses on organizational isolation of clearinghouse functions; CJIS focuses on technical isolation within shared system resources. HIPAA additionally covers administrative separation policies; CJIS additionally addresses object reuse and residual information.

column3: Health clearinghouses must implement policies protecting ePHI from unauthorized access by the larger organization.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(A) requires isolation policies for clearinghouse functions. CJIS 5.5.AC-5 requires identification and documentation of separation of duties. Both address logical and organizational separation to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
column11: Identify and document separation of duties based on specific duties operations or information systems as necessary to mitigate risk to CJI; define system access authorizations to support separation of duties.
column12: HIPAA focuses on organizational isolation of clearinghouse from parent entity; CJIS focuses on separation of duties to prevent abuse. Both address separation principles to protect sensitive data integrity and confidentiality.

column3: Implement policies and procedures for granting access to ePHI through workstations transactions programs or other mechanisms.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(B) requires policies and procedures for granting access to ePHI through various mechanisms. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires defining authorized users, assigning access authorizations, and requiring approvals for account creation. Both require formal policies and procedures for granting system access.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed; specify authorized users and access authorizations for each account; require approvals by organizational personnel with account management responsibilities for requests to create accounts.
column12: Both require documented policies and approval processes for granting access to sensitive information systems through workstations, programs, and other mechanisms.

column3: Implement policies establishing documenting reviewing and modifying user access rights to workstations transactions programs or processes.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(C) requires policies establishing, documenting, reviewing, and modifying user access rights. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires maintaining access authorizations, reviewing accounts for compliance annually, and modifying access when changes occur. Both require lifecycle management of user access rights with periodic review.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed; monitor the use of accounts; review accounts for compliance with account management requirements at least annually; modify accounts as organizational personnel change.
column12: Both require establishing, documenting, reviewing, and modifying user access rights as part of systematic access lifecycle management. HIPAA covers ePHI systems; CJIS covers CJI systems.

column3: Implement policies establishing documenting reviewing and modifying user access rights to workstations transactions programs or processes.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(4)(ii)(C) requires review and modification of user access. CJIS 5.5.AC-6(7) requires annual review of privileges assigned to non-privileged and privileged users. Both require periodic review and adjustment of access rights.
column9: 5.5.AC-6(7) Least Privilege | Review of User Privileges
column11: Review annually the privileges assigned to non-privileged and privileged users to validate the need for such privileges; reassign or remove privileges if necessary to correctly reflect organizational mission and business needs.
column12: HIPAA requires ongoing review and modification of user access rights; CJIS requires annual privilege review. Both ensure access rights remain appropriate over time.

column3: Implement a security awareness and training program for all workforce members including management.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(i) requires a security awareness and training program for all workforce members including management. CJIS 5.2.AT-2 requires security and privacy literacy training to system users including managers, senior executives, and contractors prior to access and annually thereafter. Both require comprehensive security awareness training programs covering all organizational personnel.
column11: Provide security and privacy literacy training to system users including managers senior executives and contractors as part of initial training for new users prior to accessing CJI and annually thereafter.
column12: Both require comprehensive security awareness training for all workforce members including management, with initial training and annual refreshers.

column3: Implement a security awareness and training program for all workforce members including management.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(i) requires security training. CJIS 5.2.AT-3 requires role-based security and privacy training for personnel with different roles and responsibilities. Both require training programs covering security requirements for workforce members.
column11: Provide role-based security and privacy training to personnel with defined roles and responsibilities before authorizing access and annually thereafter; update role-based training content annually.
column12: HIPAA requires a general awareness training program for all workforce members; CJIS additionally requires targeted role-based training for specific roles. CJIS is more specific in role requirements; HIPAA covers a broader workforce population.

column3: Distribute periodic security updates.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(A) requires periodic security updates/reminders. CJIS 5.2.AT-2 requires employing techniques such as displaying posters, logon screen messages, and email advisories to increase security awareness. Both require ongoing communication of security awareness information to workforce members.
column11: Employ techniques to increase the security and privacy awareness of system users including displaying posters offering security reminders displaying logon screen messages generating email advisories and conducting awareness events.
column12: Both require distributing periodic security updates and reminders to workforce members through various communication channels.

column3: Implement procedures for guarding against detecting and reporting malicious software.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(B) requires procedures for guarding against, detecting, and reporting malicious software. CJIS 5.15.SI-3 requires implementing signature-based malicious code protection mechanisms to detect and eradicate malicious code at system entry and exit points. Both require technical and procedural controls for malicious software protection.
column11: Implement signature-based malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points to detect and eradicate malicious code; automatically update mechanisms; configure to perform periodic scans and block or quarantine malicious code.
column12: Both require procedures and technical mechanisms for guarding against, detecting, and responding to malicious software. HIPAA also covers reporting requirements; CJIS provides detailed technical implementation requirements.

column3: Implement procedures for guarding against detecting and reporting malicious software.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(B) requires malware protection procedures. CJIS 5.13.4.2 requires agencies to have a process to approve specific software/applications on mobile devices. Both address malware prevention through different mechanisms for different device types.
column11: Appropriately configured MDM software is capable of checking installed applications and reporting software inventory; agencies that allow smartphones and tablets to access CJI shall have a process to approve the use of specific software or applications.
column12: HIPAA requires general malware protection procedures; CJIS 5.13.4.2 focuses on mobile device malware protection through application control. Both address malware prevention but for different contexts.

column3: Implement procedures for monitoring log-in attempts and reporting discrepancies.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(C) requires procedures for monitoring log-in attempts and reporting discrepancies. CJIS 5.4.AU-2 requires logging all successful and unsuccessful system log-on attempts. Both require systematic monitoring and recording of login activity to detect unauthorized access.
column11: Identify event types for logging within the system including all successful and unsuccessful system log-on attempts and attempts to use access create write delete and change permissions.
column12: Both require monitoring and recording of login attempts to detect unauthorized access attempts. HIPAA also requires reporting discrepancies; CJIS provides detailed event logging specifications.

column3: Implement procedures for monitoring log-in attempts and reporting discrepancies.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(C) requires login monitoring. CJIS 5.5.AC-7 requires enforcing a limit on consecutive invalid logon attempts and automatically locking accounts. Both address monitoring and control of login attempts to prevent unauthorized access.
column11: Enforce a limit of five consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user during a 15-minute time period and automatically lock the account until released by an administrator when the maximum number of unsuccessful attempts is exceeded.
column12: HIPAA focuses on monitoring and reporting login discrepancies; CJIS AC-7 focuses on automated enforcement of lockout thresholds. Both address the security risk of unauthorized login attempts.

column3: Implement procedures for creating changing and safeguarding passwords.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(D) requires procedures for creating, changing, and safeguarding passwords. CJIS 5.6.IA-5(1) requires managing memorized secret authenticators including maintaining a list of commonly-used passwords and requiring specific password characteristics. Both require formal password management policies and procedures.
column11: Control: Manage system authenticators by verifying the identity of the individual receiving the authenticator; establishing content and procedures for authenticator management; changing default authenticators prior to system use; and protecting authenticators.
column12: Both require procedures for creating, changing, and protecting passwords/authenticators. HIPAA frames this as password management; CJIS provides more detailed technical authenticator management requirements.

column3: Implement procedures for creating changing and safeguarding passwords.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(D) requires password management. CJIS 5.6.IA-5(1) requires managing memorized secret authenticators with specific standards. Both require controls over password creation, change, and protection.
column11: Maintain a list of commonly-used, expected, or compromised passwords; verify passwords are not on the list; enforce minimum password requirements; require password changes after security events.
column12: HIPAA requires general password management procedures; CJIS IA-5(1) provides specific technical requirements for memorized secret authenticators including compromise lists and complexity requirements.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to address security incidents.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(6)(i) requires policies and procedures to address security incidents. CJIS 5.3.IR-1 requires development and dissemination of agency-level incident response policy and procedures. Both require formal documented incident response policies and procedures.
column11: Develop document and disseminate agency-level incident response policy and procedures addressing purpose scope roles responsibilities management commitment coordination among organizational entities and compliance.
column12: Both require documented policies and procedures for addressing security incidents. HIPAA specifies ePHI incidents; CJIS specifies CJI-related incidents.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to address security incidents.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(6)(i) requires security incident procedures. CJIS 5.3.IR-8 requires development of a comprehensive incident response plan. Both require formal planning frameworks for responding to security incidents.
column11: Develop an incident response plan that provides the organization with a roadmap for implementing its incident response capability and describes the structure organization and high-level approach for how the incident response capability fits into the overall organization.
column12: HIPAA requires policies and procedures for addressing incidents; CJIS requires a comprehensive incident response plan with detailed organizational structure. HIPAA is broader in scope; CJIS is more prescriptive in plan content.

column3: Identify and respond to suspected or known security incidents; mitigate harmful effects; document incidents and outcomes.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(6)(ii) requires identifying, responding to, mitigating, and documenting security incidents. CJIS 5.3.IR-4 requires implementing incident handling capability covering preparation, detection, containment, eradication, and recovery. Both require comprehensive incident response activities.
column11: Implement an incident handling capability for incidents consistent with the incident response plan including preparation detection and analysis containment eradication and recovery; coordinate incident handling activities with contingency planning activities.
column12: Both require active incident response including identification, containment, and recovery. HIPAA emphasizes mitigation of harmful effects and documentation; CJIS provides more detailed handling phases.

column3: Identify and respond to suspected or known security incidents; mitigate harmful effects; document incidents and outcomes.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(6)(ii) requires documenting security incidents. CJIS 5.3.IR-5 requires tracking and documenting incidents. Both require maintaining records of security incidents.
column11: Track and document incidents; maintain records about each incident the status of the incident and other pertinent information necessary for forensics as well as evaluating incident details trends and handling.
column12: Both require documentation of security incidents including status and outcomes. HIPAA specifies documenting outcomes; CJIS specifies maintaining forensic records.

column3: Identify and respond to suspected or known security incidents; mitigate harmful effects; document incidents and outcomes.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(6)(ii) requires reporting of security incidents. CJIS 5.3.IR-6 requires reporting suspected incidents within one hour of discovery. Both require timely reporting of security incidents to appropriate personnel.
column11: Require personnel to report suspected incidents to the organizational incident response capability immediately but not to exceed one hour after discovery; report incident information to organizational personnel with incident handling responsibilities.
column12: HIPAA requires reporting and mitigation of incidents; CJIS specifies a one-hour reporting timeframe. Both require timely incident reporting. HIPAA additionally requires mitigation of harmful effects; CJIS additionally specifies notification to the CSO.

column3: Establish policies and procedures for responding to emergencies or other occurrences that damage systems containing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(i) requires policies and procedures for responding to emergencies or occurrences that damage ePHI systems. CJIS 5.18.CP-1 requires development of agency-level contingency planning policy and procedures. Both require formal contingency planning policies and procedures.
column11: Develop document and disseminate organizational personnel with contingency planning responsibilities agency-level contingency planning policy and procedures addressing purpose scope roles responsibilities management commitment and compliance.
column12: Both require documented contingency planning policies and procedures for responding to system disruptions. HIPAA focuses on ePHI system protection; CJIS provides broader contingency planning requirements.

column3: Establish policies and procedures for responding to emergencies or other occurrences that damage systems containing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(i) requires contingency planning. CJIS 5.18.CP-2 requires development of a comprehensive contingency plan addressing essential functions, recovery objectives, and restoration. Both require formal contingency plans.
column11: Develop a contingency plan for the system that identifies essential mission and business functions provides recovery objectives and restoration priorities addresses contingency roles and responsibilities and addresses maintaining essential functions despite disruption.
column12: HIPAA requires contingency policies and procedures for emergency response; CJIS requires a detailed contingency plan with recovery objectives. HIPAA covers emergency and disaster scenarios; CJIS provides more prescriptive plan content requirements.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to create and maintain retrievable exact copies of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(A) requires procedures to create and maintain retrievable exact copies of ePHI. CJIS 5.18.CP-9 requires conducting backups of user-level and system-level information and protecting the CIA of backup information. Both require systematic backup procedures for sensitive information.
column11: Conduct backups of user-level information system-level information and system documentation contained in operational systems as required by the contingency plans; protect the confidentiality integrity and availability of backup information.
column12: Both require creating and maintaining backup copies of sensitive information systems. HIPAA specifies exact retrievable copies of ePHI; CJIS specifies backup of user-level and system-level information with CIA protection.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to create and maintain retrievable exact copies of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(A) requires backup procedures. CJIS 5.18.CP-9(1) requires testing backup information to verify media reliability and information integrity. Both address maintaining reliable backups.
column9: 5.18.CP-9(1) System Backup | Testing For Reliability And Integrity
column11: Test backup information as required by the contingency plans to verify media reliability and information integrity.
column12: HIPAA requires creating and maintaining backups; CJIS additionally requires testing backup reliability. CJIS provides more specific quality assurance requirements for backups.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to restore loss of data.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(B) requires procedures for restoring lost data. CJIS 5.18.CP-10 requires provision for recovery and reconstitution of the system to a known state after disruption. Both require formal data and system recovery procedures.
column11: Provide for the recovery and reconstitution of the system to a known state within the timeframe as required by the contingency plans after a disruption compromise or failure.
column12: Both require procedures for restoring systems and data after loss or disruption. HIPAA specifies data restoration; CJIS covers full system recovery and reconstitution to a known state.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to restore loss of data.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(B) requires data recovery procedures. CJIS 5.18.CP-7 requires establishing an alternate processing site to enable transfer and resumption of operations. Both address disaster recovery capabilities.
column11: Establish an alternate processing site including necessary agreements to permit the transfer and resumption of operations for essential mission and business functions within the time period specified in the contingency plan when the primary site is unavailable.
column12: HIPAA focuses on data restoration procedures; CJIS additionally covers alternate processing sites. HIPAA is procedural; CJIS provides infrastructure requirements for disaster recovery.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to enable continuation of critical business processes while operating in emergency mode.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(C) requires procedures for continuing critical business processes while operating in emergency mode. CJIS 5.18.CP-2(3) requires planning for resumption of essential mission and business functions within 24 hours of contingency plan activation. Both require business continuity planning for emergency operations.
column9: 5.18.CP-2(3) Contingency Plan | Resume Mission And Business Functions
column11: Plan for the resumption of essential mission and business functions within twenty-four hours of contingency plan activation.
column12: Both require planning to maintain or quickly resume critical business functions during emergency operations. HIPAA covers ePHI protection during emergency mode; CJIS specifies a 24-hour recovery time objective.

column3: Establish and implement procedures to enable continuation of critical business processes while operating in emergency mode.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(C) requires emergency mode operation plans. CJIS 5.18.CP-8 requires establishing alternate telecommunications services for resumption during primary capability unavailability. Both address maintaining operations during emergencies.
column11: Establish alternate telecommunications services including necessary agreements to permit the resumption of system operations for essential mission and business functions within the time period defined in the system contingency plans when primary telecommunications capabilities are unavailable.
column12: HIPAA covers continuation of critical business processes during emergencies; CJIS covers telecommunications continuity specifically. HIPAA is broader; CJIS addresses a specific critical infrastructure component.

column3: Implement procedures for periodic testing and revision of contingency plans.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(D) requires periodic testing and revision of contingency plans. CJIS 5.18.CP-4 requires testing the contingency plan annually and initiating corrective actions based on results. Both require regular testing and updating of contingency plans.
column11: Test the contingency plan for the system annually using checklists walk-through and tabletop exercises or simulations to determine the effectiveness of the plan; review test results; initiate corrective actions if needed.
column12: Both require periodic testing of contingency plans and updating them based on test results. HIPAA requires testing and revision; CJIS specifies annual testing with defined test methods.

column3: Assess the relative criticality of specific applications and data in support of contingency plan components.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(E) requires assessment of the relative criticality of specific applications and data for contingency planning. CJIS 5.18.CP-2(8) requires identifying critical system assets supporting essential mission and business functions. Both require analysis and prioritization of critical systems and data for continuity purposes.
column11: Identify critical system assets supporting essential mission and business functions so that additional controls can be employed to ensure organizational mission and business functions can continue during contingency operations.
column12: Both require identifying and prioritizing critical applications and data assets to support contingency planning. HIPAA focuses on ePHI systems; CJIS focuses on CJI mission-critical assets.

column3: Assess the relative criticality of specific applications and data in support of contingency plan components.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(7)(ii)(E) requires criticality analysis for contingency planning. CJIS 5.19.RA-9 requires criticality analysis of information system components during the system development lifecycle. Both require systematic assessment of component criticality.
column11: Identify critical system components and functions by performing a criticality analysis for information system components containing or processing CJI at the planning design development testing implementation and maintenance stages of the system development life cycle.
column12: HIPAA requires criticality analysis specifically for contingency planning; CJIS requires criticality analysis across the full system development lifecycle. Both identify critical assets requiring additional protection.

column3: Perform a periodic technical and nontechnical evaluation based upon implemented standards and changes affecting security of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(8) requires periodic technical and nontechnical evaluation of the extent to which security policies meet requirements. CJIS 5.11.2 requires CSAs to audit CJAs at least triennially for compliance with applicable statutes, regulations, and policies. Both require periodic formal evaluation of security program compliance.
column11: Each CSA shall at a minimum triennially audit all CJAs and NCJAs with direct access to the state system to ensure compliance with applicable statutes regulations and policies.
column12: HIPAA requires both technical and nontechnical self-evaluation triggered by changes; CJIS requires formal third-party audits at minimum triennial frequency. Both require periodic evaluation of security compliance.

column3: Perform a periodic technical and nontechnical evaluation based upon implemented standards and changes affecting security of ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(a)(8) requires periodic security evaluation. CJIS 5.19.RA-3 requires conducting risk assessments. Both require systematic periodic evaluation of security status.
column11: Conduct a risk assessment including identifying threats to and vulnerabilities in the system; determining the likelihood and magnitude of harm; determine the risk to organizational operations and assets individuals other organizations and the Nation.
column12: HIPAA requires technical and nontechnical evaluation of policy compliance; CJIS requires formal risk assessment. Both require periodic systematic security evaluation. HIPAA is triggered by environmental changes; CJIS provides specific assessment criteria.

column2: Business Associate Contracts and Other Arrangements
column3: Covered entities may permit business associates to handle ePHI only with satisfactory assurances that BAs will safeguard information.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(b)(1) requires covered entities to obtain satisfactory assurances from business associates that they will appropriately safeguard ePHI. CJIS 5.1.1.5 requires private contractors to meet the same security criteria as governmental agencies and sign the CJIS Security Addendum. Both require contractual assurances from third parties handling sensitive information.
column9: 5.1.1.5 Private Contractor User Agreements and CJIS Security Addendum
column11: Private contractors who perform criminal justice functions shall meet the same training and certification criteria required by governmental agencies; all private contractors shall acknowledge and abide by all aspects of the CJIS Security Addendum.
column12: Both require contractual assurances from third parties handling sensitive information that they will implement appropriate safeguards. HIPAA uses BACs; CJIS uses the Security Addendum.

column2: Business Associate Contracts and Other Arrangements
column3: Covered entities may permit business associates to handle ePHI only with satisfactory assurances that BAs will safeguard information.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(b)(1) requires satisfactory assurances from business associates. CJIS 5.1.2 requires monitoring and reviewing services provided by service providers. Both require oversight of third-party entities handling sensitive information.
column9: 5.1.2 Monitoring Review and Delivery of Services
column11: The services reports and records provided by the service provider shall be regularly monitored and reviewed; the CJA shall maintain sufficient overall control and visibility into all security aspects including identification of vulnerabilities and incident reporting/response.
column12: HIPAA requires obtaining assurances before allowing BA access; CJIS requires ongoing monitoring after establishing service provider relationships. Both address third-party security management. HIPAA focuses on contractual assurances; CJIS focuses on ongoing oversight.

column3: Document satisfactory assurances required from business associates through a written contract.
column6: HIPAA 164.308(b)(4) requires documenting satisfactory assurances from BAs through a written contract meeting requirements of §164.314(a). CJIS 5.1.1.3 requires any CJA receiving access to CJI to enter a signed written agreement with the appropriate signatory authority. Both require written contractual agreements governing third-party access to sensitive information.
column11: Any CJA receiving access to CJI shall enter into a signed written agreement with the appropriate signatory authority of the CSA providing the access; the written agreement shall specify the FBI CJIS systems and services and the FBI CJIS Division policies to which the agency must adhere.
column12: Both require written contracts/agreements as the formal mechanism for documenting security obligations of third parties accessing sensitive information. HIPAA covers BA relationships; CJIS covers CJA agreements.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to limit physical access to electronic information systems and facilities while ensuring authorized access.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(1) requires policies and procedures to limit physical access to ePHI systems and facilities while ensuring authorized access. CJIS 5.9.PE-3 requires enforcing physical access authorizations and controlling ingress and egress to facilities. Both require comprehensive physical access control systems for facilities housing sensitive information systems.
column11: Enforce physical access authorizations by verifying individual access authorizations before granting access; control ingress and egress to the facility; control access to non-publicly accessible areas; escort visitors and control visitor activity.
column12: Both require physical access controls limiting unauthorized physical access to facilities containing sensitive information systems while permitting authorized access.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to limit physical access to electronic information systems and facilities while ensuring authorized access.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(1) requires facility access controls. CJIS 5.9.PE-2 requires developing and maintaining a list of individuals with authorized access to the facility. Both require formal authorization and management of physical facility access.
column11: Develop approve and maintain a list of individuals with authorized access to the facility where the system resides; issue authorization credentials for facility access; review the access list annually and when personnel changes occur.
column12: HIPAA requires policies limiting physical access; CJIS PE-2 requires a specific authorization list and credentials. Both require managing who has authorized physical access to facilities. CJIS provides more specific list management requirements.

column3: Establish procedures for facility access supporting restoration of lost data under disaster recovery plan in emergency.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(i) requires procedures allowing facility access to support disaster recovery and emergency operations. CJIS 5.18.CP-7 requires establishing alternate processing sites and agreements for transfer and resumption of operations. Both require planning for facility and system access during emergency and recovery operations.
column11: Establish an alternate processing site including necessary agreements to permit the transfer and resumption of operations for essential mission and business functions when the primary processing site is unavailable.
column12: HIPAA focuses on maintaining facility access for recovery operations at the primary site; CJIS focuses on alternate processing sites. Both address operational continuity during emergencies but from different perspectives.

column3: Establish procedures for facility access supporting restoration of lost data under disaster recovery plan in emergency.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(i) requires contingency operations procedures for facility access. CJIS 5.9.PE-3 requires controlling ingress and egress with verification procedures. Both require procedures for managing facility access.
column11: Enforce physical access authorizations by verifying individual access authorizations before granting access to the facility; control ingress and egress to the facility using agency-implemented procedures and controls.
column12: HIPAA focuses on emergency/recovery access procedures; CJIS focuses on routine and controlled physical access. Both require formal procedures governing physical access to facilities.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to safeguard the facility and equipment from unauthorized physical access tampering and theft.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(ii) requires policies to safeguard facilities and equipment from unauthorized access, tampering, and theft. CJIS 5.9.PE-1 requires developing agency-level physical and environmental protection policy. Both require formal physical security policies for facilities housing sensitive information systems.
column11: Develop document and disseminate organizational personnel with physical and environmental protection responsibilities: agency-level physical and environmental protection policy and procedures addressing purpose scope roles responsibilities management commitment and compliance.
column12: Both require documented physical security policies and procedures for safeguarding facilities from unauthorized access, tampering, and theft.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to safeguard the facility and equipment from unauthorized physical access tampering and theft.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(ii) requires facility security plans. CJIS 5.9.PE-6 requires monitoring physical access to detect and respond to physical security incidents. Both require physical security controls for facilities.
column11: Monitor physical access to the facility where the system resides to detect and respond to physical security incidents; review physical access logs quarterly; coordinate results of reviews with the organizational incident response capability.
column12: HIPAA requires policies and procedures to safeguard facilities; CJIS PE-6 requires ongoing monitoring and review. Both address facility security but from different angles. HIPAA covers planning; CJIS covers ongoing monitoring.

column3: Implement procedures to control and validate access to facilities based on role or function including visitor control.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(iii) requires procedures to control and validate facility access based on role or function including visitor control. CJIS 5.9.PE-3 requires escorting visitors, controlling visitor activity, and verifying access authorizations before granting entry. Both require role-based facility access validation with specific visitor controls.
column11: Enforce physical access authorizations by verifying individual access authorizations before granting access to the facility; escort visitors and control visitor activity in all physically secure locations; secure keys combinations and other physical access devices.
column12: Both require procedures to validate and control facility access based on role or function with specific visitor control requirements.

column3: Implement procedures to control and validate access to facilities based on role or function including visitor control.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(iii) requires visitor access control. CJIS 5.9.PE-8 requires maintaining visitor access records for one year. Both require managing and recording visitor access to facilities.
column11: Maintain visitor access records to the facility where the system resides for one year; review visitor access records quarterly; report anomalies in visitor access records to organizational personnel with physical and environmental protection responsibilities.
column12: HIPAA requires procedures to control and validate visitor access; CJIS requires maintaining and reviewing visitor records. Both address visitor management. CJIS adds specific record retention and review requirements.

column3: Implement policies to document repairs and modifications to physical components of a facility related to security.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(a)(2)(iv) requires policies to document security-related repairs and modifications to facility physical components. CJIS 5.9.PE-16 requires authorizing and controlling information system-related components entering and exiting the facility and maintaining records. Both require documentation of physical changes to facility security components.
column9: 5.9.PE-16 Delivery and Removal of Information System Components
column11: Authorize and control information system-related components entering and exiting the facility; maintain records of the system components.
column12: Both require documentation and control records for physical changes and components related to security in facilities housing sensitive information systems.

column3: Implement policies specifying proper functions and physical environment of workstations accessing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(b) requires policies specifying proper functions and physical environment of workstations accessing ePHI. CJIS 5.5.AC-8 requires displaying system use notifications before granting access specifying authorized use and monitoring. Both require defined authorized use policies for systems and workstations.
column11: Display a system use notification message to users before granting access to the system that states users are accessing a restricted information system and that unauthorized use is prohibited and subject to criminal and civil penalties.
column12: HIPAA requires policies governing workstation functions and physical environment; CJIS requires system use notification banners. Both define authorized use of workstations/systems but through different mechanisms.

column3: Implement policies specifying proper functions and physical environment of workstations accessing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(b) requires physical environment specifications for workstations. CJIS 5.9.PE-5 requires controlling physical access to output from monitors, printers, and other output devices. Both address the physical environment of workstations and protection of information displayed.
column11: Control physical access to output from monitors printers scanners audio devices facsimile machines and copiers to prevent unauthorized individuals from obtaining the output.
column12: HIPAA addresses the physical surroundings of workstations; CJIS addresses physical access to workstation output. Both protect against unauthorized viewing or access to sensitive information from workstations.

column3: Implement physical safeguards for all workstations accessing ePHI to restrict access to authorized users.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(c) requires physical safeguards for workstations to restrict access to authorized users. CJIS 5.9.PE-3 requires controlling physical access using physical access devices and enforcing access authorizations. Both require physical access controls protecting workstations from unauthorized use.
column11: Control access to areas within the facility designated as non-publicly accessible by implementing physical access devices including keys locks combinations biometric readers and card readers; escort visitors and control visitor activity.
column12: Both require physical safeguards restricting workstation access to authorized users. HIPAA specifies workstations accessing ePHI; CJIS specifies access control for facilities housing CJI systems.

column3: Implement physical safeguards for all workstations accessing ePHI to restrict access to authorized users.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(c) requires physical workstation security. CJIS 5.9.PE-17 covers alternate work site physical security requirements. Both address physical security of work environments housing access to sensitive information.
column11: Determine and document all alternate facilities or locations allowed for use by employees; employ physical and technical security controls at alternate work sites comparable to those at primary sites.
column12: HIPAA requires physical safeguards for all workstations; CJIS PE-17 addresses physical security at alternate work sites. Both address physical protection of workstations. HIPAA covers all workstations; CJIS specifically addresses alternate/remote work locations.

column3: Implement policies governing receipt and removal of hardware and media containing ePHI into and out of a facility.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(1) requires policies governing receipt and removal of hardware and electronic media containing ePHI. CJIS 5.9.PE-16 requires authorizing and controlling information system components entering and exiting the facility with records maintenance. Both require controlled movement of hardware and media carrying sensitive information.
column9: 5.9.PE-16 Delivery and Removal of Information System Components
column11: Authorize and control information system-related components entering and exiting the facility; maintain records of the system components.
column12: Both require policies governing the receipt and removal of hardware and media from facilities, with documentation of movements.

column3: Implement policies governing receipt and removal of hardware and media containing ePHI into and out of a facility.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(1) requires device and media controls. CJIS 5.8.MP-7 requires restricting use of digital media on agency systems and prohibiting personally owned media. Both require policies governing media use in sensitive information systems.
column11: Restrict the use of digital and non-digital media on agency owned systems approved for CJI storage processing or transmission; prohibit the use of personally owned digital media devices on all agency owned or controlled systems.
column12: HIPAA requires policies governing receipt and removal of hardware and media; CJIS requires policies governing what media may be used on systems. Both control media in facilities. HIPAA covers movement of media; CJIS covers types of permitted media.

column3: Implement policies addressing final disposition of ePHI and hardware or media on which it is stored.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(2)(i) requires policies addressing final disposition of ePHI and hardware/media storing it. CJIS 5.8.MP-6 requires sanitizing or destroying digital and non-digital media prior to disposal or release for reuse. Both require secure disposal of media containing sensitive information.
column11: Sanitize or destroy digital and non-digital media prior to disposal release out of agency control or release for reuse using overwrite technology at least three times or degauss digital media; destroy inoperable digital media.
column12: Both require secure disposal or destruction of media containing sensitive information. HIPAA covers ePHI; CJIS covers CJI. Both specify that media must be sanitized before disposal or reuse.

column3: Implement procedures for removal of ePHI from electronic media before media are made available for re-use.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(2)(ii) requires procedures for removing ePHI from electronic media before re-use. CJIS 5.8.MP-6 requires sanitizing media prior to release for reuse using overwrite technology or degaussing. Both require media sanitization before re-use to prevent data remnants.
column11: Sanitize digital media prior to disposal release out of agency control or release for reuse using overwrite technology at least three times or degauss digital media prior to disposal or release for reuse by unauthorized individuals.
column12: Both require removal of sensitive data from electronic media before reuse through sanitization or overwriting. HIPAA covers ePHI; CJIS covers CJI.

column3: Maintain a record of the movements of hardware and electronic media and any person responsible.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(2)(iii) requires maintaining records of hardware and electronic media movements and responsible persons. CJIS 5.8.MP-5 requires maintaining accountability for system media during transport and documenting activities associated with transport. Both require tracking records for hardware and media movements.
column11: Protect and control digital and non-digital media during transport; maintain accountability for system media during transport outside of the physically secure location or controlled areas; document activities associated with the transport of system media.
column12: Both require maintaining records of hardware and media movements and accountability for the persons responsible. HIPAA covers movements within and out of facilities; CJIS covers media transport outside secure locations.

column3: Create a retrievable exact copy of ePHI before movement of equipment.
column6: HIPAA 164.310(d)(2)(iv) requires creating a retrievable exact copy of ePHI before moving equipment. CJIS 5.18.CP-9 requires conducting backups of user-level information in operational systems as required by contingency plans. Both require backup creation before system changes that could risk data loss.
column11: Conduct backups of user-level information contained in operational systems for essential business functions as required by the contingency plans; protect the confidentiality integrity and availability of backup information.
column12: HIPAA requires backup before equipment movement; CJIS requires backups per contingency plan schedule. Both address backup of sensitive information to prevent loss. HIPAA is triggered by equipment movement; CJIS follows contingency plan schedule.

column3: Implement technical policies and procedures allowing access only to authorized persons or software programs.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(1) requires technical policies and procedures for ePHI systems to allow access only to authorized persons or software programs. CJIS 5.5.AC-3 requires enforcement of approved authorizations for logical access to information and system resources. Both require technical enforcement of access authorizations.
column11: Enforce approved authorizations for logical access to information and system resources in accordance with applicable access control policies.
column12: Both require technical controls ensuring only authorized users and processes can access sensitive information systems. HIPAA specifies ePHI systems; CJIS specifies CJI systems.

column3: Implement technical policies and procedures allowing access only to authorized persons or software programs.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(1) requires access control. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires defining authorized users, group and role membership, and access authorizations for each account. Both require systematic management of who may access sensitive information systems.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed; specify authorized users group and role membership and access authorizations for each account; authorize access to the system based on a valid access authorization and intended system usage.
column12: HIPAA requires technical access control limiting access to authorized persons; CJIS provides detailed account management to implement those authorizations. Both address controlling system access to authorized entities.

column3: Assign a unique name and/or number for identifying and tracking user identity.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(i) requires assigning unique names and/or numbers for identifying and tracking user identity. CJIS 5.6.IA-2 requires uniquely identifying and authenticating organizational users. Both require unique identification of users accessing sensitive information systems.
column11: Uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users and associate that unique identification with processes acting on behalf of those users.
column12: Both require unique identification of all users accessing sensitive information systems. HIPAA requires assignment of unique identifiers; CJIS requires unique identification and authentication.

column3: Assign a unique name and/or number for identifying and tracking user identity.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(i) requires unique user identification. CJIS 5.6.IA-4 requires managing system identifiers and preventing reuse of identifiers for one year. Both require unique and managed user identifiers.
column11: Manage system identifiers by receiving authorization to assign identifiers; selecting identifiers that uniquely identify individuals groups roles services or devices; assigning identifiers to intended individuals; and preventing reuse of identifiers for one year.
column12: HIPAA requires assigning unique user IDs; CJIS provides detailed identifier management including prevention of identifier reuse. CJIS provides more specific identifier lifecycle management requirements.

column3: Establish and implement procedures for obtaining necessary ePHI during an emergency.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(ii) requires procedures for obtaining necessary ePHI during an emergency. CJIS 5.18.CP-2(3) requires planning for resumption of essential mission and business functions within 24 hours of contingency plan activation. Both require emergency access planning for critical information.
column9: 5.18.CP-2(3) Contingency Plan | Resume Mission And Business Functions
column11: Plan for the resumption of essential mission and business functions within twenty-four hours of contingency plan activation.
column12: HIPAA requires specific procedures for accessing ePHI during emergencies; CJIS requires planning for 24-hour resumption of essential functions. HIPAA focuses specifically on ePHI access; CJIS covers broader business function resumption.

column3: Establish and implement procedures for obtaining necessary ePHI during an emergency.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(ii) requires emergency access procedures. CJIS 5.5.AC-2 requires account management including emergency accounts. Both address access management during emergency situations.
column11: Define and document the types of accounts allowed including emergency accounts; create enable modify disable and remove accounts in accordance with agency policy; automatically remove temporary and emergency accounts within 72 hours.
column12: HIPAA requires emergency access procedures for ePHI; CJIS manages emergency accounts as part of overall account management. Both address emergency system access scenarios.

column3: Implement electronic procedures that terminate an electronic session after predetermined period of inactivity.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(iii) requires electronic procedures to terminate sessions after predetermined inactivity. CJIS 5.5.AC-11 requires initiating a device lock after a maximum of 30 minutes of inactivity. Both require automatic session termination or locking after periods of inactivity.
column11: Prevent further access to the system by initiating a device lock after a maximum of 30 minutes of inactivity and requiring the user to initiate a device lock before leaving the system unattended; retain the device lock until the user reestablishes access using established identification and authentication procedures.
column12: Both require automatic termination or locking of sessions after a defined period of inactivity to prevent unauthorized access to unattended sessions.

column3: Implement electronic procedures that terminate an electronic session after predetermined period of inactivity.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(iii) requires automatic logoff. CJIS 5.5.AC-12 requires automatically terminating a user session after logout. Both require automatic session termination.
column11: Automatically terminate a user session after a user has been logged out.
column12: HIPAA requires session termination after inactivity; CJIS AC-12 requires termination after logout. Both ensure sessions are properly terminated. HIPAA focuses on inactivity timeout; CJIS focuses on complete session termination.

column3: Implement a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt electronic protected health information.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(iv) requires a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt ePHI. CJIS 5.10.SC-28 requires protecting the confidentiality and integrity of CJI at rest using FIPS 140-3 certified cryptographic modules. Both require encryption mechanisms to protect sensitive information stored in information systems.
column11: Protect the confidentiality and integrity of CJI at rest using cryptographic modules which are certified FIPS 140-3 with a symmetric cipher key of at least 128-bit strength.
column12: Both require encryption mechanisms to protect sensitive information stored in information systems. HIPAA specifies ePHI; CJIS specifies CJI with specific FIPS 140-3 standards.

column3: Implement a mechanism to encrypt and decrypt electronic protected health information.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(a)(2)(iv) requires encryption and decryption mechanisms. CJIS 5.10.SC-28(1) requires cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure and modification of CJI at rest. Both require cryptographic protection of sensitive information at rest.
column9: 5.10.SC-28(1) Protection of Information at Rest | Cryptographic Protection
column11: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure and modification of CJI at rest on information systems and digital media outside physically secure locations.
column12: HIPAA requires encryption/decryption capability for ePHI; CJIS requires specific cryptographic mechanisms for CJI at rest outside physically secure locations. Both require encryption of sensitive information at rest.

column3: Implement hardware software and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems containing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(b) requires hardware, software, and procedural mechanisms to record and examine activity in ePHI systems. CJIS 5.4.AU-12 requires providing audit record generation capability for required event types. Both require technical and procedural audit mechanisms for sensitive information systems.
column11: Provide audit record generation capability for the event types the system is capable of auditing on all systems generating required audit logs; generate audit records for defined event types that include the audit record content defined in AU-3.
column12: Both require implementation of audit mechanisms to record and examine activity in sensitive information systems. HIPAA covers ePHI systems; CJIS specifies detailed event types and audit record content.

column3: Implement hardware software and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems containing ePHI.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(b) requires audit controls. CJIS 5.4.AU-3 requires audit records to contain specific content establishing what when where source outcome and identity for each event. Both require audit records with sufficient content to support review and investigation.
column11: Ensure that audit records contain information that establishes: what type of event occurred; when the event occurred; where the event occurred; source of the event; outcome of the event; and identity of any individuals subjects or objects associated with the event.
column12: HIPAA requires mechanisms to record and examine activity; CJIS specifies detailed content requirements for audit records. HIPAA is broader; CJIS provides specific content standards for audit records.

column3: Implement policies and procedures to protect ePHI from improper alteration or destruction.
column6: HIPAA 164.312(c)(1) requires policies and procedures to protect ePHI from improper alteration or destruction. CJIS 5.15.SI-7 requires employing integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to software, firmware, and information systems. Both require controls protecting the integrity of sensitive information.
column11: Employ integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to software firmware and information systems that contain or process CJI; take actions when unauthorized changes are detected including notifying organizational personnel and implementing incident response procedures.
